skill.name: unity-editor
skill.description: Control a Unity Editor session through unityctl for project status, play mode, asset refresh, logs, scenes, tests, screenshots, and debugging scripts.
skill.summary: Control and debug Unity Editor projects from an AI coding session using unityctl.
skill.author: DirtybitGames

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command examples, but review shows they are Markdown instructions for expected unityctl operations, not Ruby runtime backticks. The skill intentionally enables Unity CLI control and arbitrary C# execution in the editor, which is legitimate for trusted projects but can modify project state or inspect local data. No evidence found for prompt injection, network exfiltration, screenshot upload, or weak cryptography.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary C# Execution in Unity Editor
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run arbitrary C# through unityctl script execution. This is a legitimate debugging feature, but it can inspect or change Unity project state and should be limited to trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes executing arbitrary C# via Roslyn and gives multiple script execution examples. The context is legitimate Unity debugging, so this is elevated risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Unity CLI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run unityctl commands that start and stop services, launch Unity, refresh assets, run tests, load scenes, and enter play mode. These commands are expected for the skill, but they can alter local editor and project state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static command findings are real command instructions, but they are Markdown examples rather than hidden shell execution. The risk is operational impact from an AI agent running the documented CLI commands.

low_findings.title: Local Screenshot Capture Privacy Risk
low_findings.description: The static upload finding is a false positive because the file only documents screenshot capture and shows no upload destination. Screenshot capture can still expose project visuals or editor state if used without user intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present, but no upload or network behavior appears in the reviewed file. The remaining concern is local privacy exposure from screenshots.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert at the frontmatter description is a false positive. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, encryption API, or related command is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive text about remote Unity Editor control. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage in the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Editor Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented unityctl script execute command can run user-provided C# inside the editor. This pattern is powerful and should require clear user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and direct-code option are explicitly documented. Legitimate debugging context reduces confidence that it is malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: AI-Initiated Local Tool Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill encourages running local unityctl commands that control the bridge, Unity Editor, tests, logs, scenes, and play mode. These are normal for this skill but should be visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and affect local tools. I found no obfuscation, persistence, or exfiltration behavior.

content.user_title: Control Unity Editor from AI Tools
content.value_statement: Unity debugging often requires switching between code, editor state, logs, tests, and play mode. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a unityctl workflow for controlled editor automation.
actual_capabilities: Starts and stops the unityctl bridge daemon.
Launches and stops Unity Editor through unityctl.
Refreshes assets to compile C# script changes.
Enters and exits play mode, then reads Unity logs.
Lists and loads scenes, runs edit mode or play mode tests.
Executes C# debugging scripts and captures screenshots.
limitations: Requires unityctl, Unity Editor, and a compatible project setup.
Works only when the bridge and editor connection are available.
Arbitrary C# execution should be used only in trusted projects.
Commands can change editor state, scenes, assets, and test output.

use_cases.title: Debug Runtime Behavior
use_cases.description: Enter play mode, inspect logs, run focused C# probes, and verify fixes without leaving the coding session.

use_cases.title: Automate Editor Checks
use_cases.description: Refresh assets, run edit mode tests, run play mode tests, and collect failures after code changes.

use_cases.title: Inspect Project State
use_cases.description: Check editor status, list scenes, load a target scene, and capture screenshots for visual review.

prompt_templates.title: Check Unity Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether Unity Editor and the unityctl bridge are available for this project. Report any missing setup steps before running more commands.

prompt_templates.title: Compile Recent Script Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Refresh Unity assets and summarize any compile errors. Do not enter play mode until compilation succeeds.

prompt_templates.title: Run Play Mode Debug Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Enter play mode, reproduce the issue if possible, read recent Unity logs with stack traces, then exit play mode and summarize likely causes.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Scene with C# Probe
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a temporary C# inspection script for the current Unity scene, explain what it will read, ask before execution, then summarize the result.

output_examples.input: Check whether the editor is ready.
output_examples.output: The report states whether Unity is running, whether the bridge is connected, and what setup command is needed next.

output_examples.input: Refresh assets after my C# change.
output: Compilation completed successfully, or errors are grouped by file and message.
The response recommends whether play mode is safe to start.

output_examples.input: Find why the player object is missing.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a small scene inspection, asks before running it, then summarizes the object lookup result and next debugging step.

best_practices: Run status checks before editor control commands.
Refresh assets after C# edits and fix compile errors first.
Review any generated C# script before allowing execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run editor scripts in untrusted projects.
Do not enter play mode when compilation is failing.
Do not capture screenshots without clear user intent.

faq.question: Does this skill require Unity to be installed?
faq.answer: Yes. It also requires unityctl and a Unity project that can connect to the bridge.

faq.question: Can it run Unity tests?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents edit mode tests and play mode tests through unityctl.

faq.question: Can it change my project?
faq.answer: Yes. Asset refresh, scene loading, play mode, and C# scripts can change editor or project state.

faq.question: Is arbitrary C# execution safe?
faq.answer: It is powerful and should be used only in trusted projects after reviewing the intended script.

faq.question: Does it upload screenshots?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file documents local screenshot capture, not upload behavior.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
